segments.text: BAM! Mr. Tarrou. In this video I intend on running through a very large set of examples
for you. That is why this video is so long. Of reading some real life situations or at
least some sentences and converting them into algebraic expressions. Now these can
vary from the ridiculously easy to... yeah this is pretty complex. Depending on your
textbook, at this point just converting from words to expressions might seem like overly
easy and not real purposeful or have real meaning. But this basic idea of taking real
language and writing it into a math equation is what algebra is all about. Taking real
life scenarios. Ultimately of course we are trying to... you know no one talks
as in x's and y's. We talk in words and we find area for carpet that we are
trying to figure out how to solve this problem or this real situation. How much does this
carpet cost that is in this room? Well I have to find square footage and that is
length times width and I have to... blah blah blah. So getting from words to algebra
is a really important idea. So let's start off with some pretty easy examples. Three
more than a number. Well a number. What number? I don't know. Well that is what
the variable is. The variable is a letter that represents an unknown number. So let's
just call this a number. Let's just call this... I don't know... b. So three more
than a number. Well more than means add. The more than and the less than... when
those two words are together they also imply that you need to change the order
that you see the stuff in the sentence. Change the order of the sentence around. In other
words if I have got twenty dollars and I need three more than that it is going to
be twenty plus three which is twenty three. Well who cares. Three plus twenty is twenty
three as well. But if I have twenty dollars and I have... for some reason I need less
than that. I need three less than twenty. Well three minus twenty is negative seventeen.
That doesn't make any sense. It is twenty minus three. When you have these two words
together you need to know more means add, less means subtract. But those two words
together means that you need to change the order of the sentence. What you see in the
sentence. So a number, this unknown value, needs to come first and it is less... you
know three. It is three less than this unknown value of b. How about five less
than twice a again unknown value. I don't care what letter you pick. Twice a number is a
multiplication of two. So twice a number is going to be 2b and then five... oh excuse me
this is more than. Sorry about that. So five less than twice a number is take five away
from it. The quotient. Well quotient is division. The quotient, I am dividing the sum. Sum
is addition. So I am going to add a couple of numbers. I am going to add six and this
unknown value. So the quotient is division. The quotient of the sum of x and a number.
So it is implying that I need to add these two numbers before I do this division. So
I am going to show that as six plus an unknown value of b. I don't really need the parenthesis
there because a fraction bar acts as a grouping symbol. But for good measure I am
adding them in. So the quotient of the sum of six and a number and three. So it
is the quotient of all of this and three. So it is all of this divided by three.
And there is our third example. The difference. The word difference means subtract. So the
subtraction of, the difference of. Product means multiply. So I am going to take a
couple of numbers now again, it is kind of like the third example, and multiply.
I want the difference of the product of an unknown value in seven. So I want to
So there is the product of seven and a number. That is the difference. I want the difference
of the product of a number and four. I want the difference of this and four. So there
is the difference of seven and a number and four. Seven and b minus four. You
are selling CDs. Ok. What is your profit after selling a certain number of discs,
an unknown value of discs for $8 a piece when it costs you $300 to have them
produced? Well to answer this question you have to understand what profit is.
Profit is what you have left after like a store. You know a store doesn't get
this stuff for free. The store buys stuff. So maybe the store bought
something for $50 and then sold it for $60. Well their profit is the
difference between what they sold it for and how much it cost them. So it is
going to be 60 minus 50. So profit is the difference between what you bring in
and what something costs you. Now I am oversimplifying this because stores have
electricity and rental and all that other good stuff, but just a simple
example of profit. So what is your profit after selling you know a certain
number of discs at $8 a piece? Well how are we going to figure out how
much you made? Well if you sold two discs it was two times eight which is
$16. If you sold three discs that would be 3 times 8 or 24. So you are going to figure out how much
you are bringing in by doing eight times the number of discs that you sold.
And notice how I said eight times the number of discs that you sold. I didn't say 8x.
That is another reason why people never think they do algebras. Because we don't
talk in x's and y's. We talk in words. How much did you make? 8 times how
many I sold. Great. But I didn't get those for free. It cost me $300. So
from what I brought in I have to subtract for profit, for the idea of
profit, I have to subtract how much those discs cost me in the first place.
So I am selling them at $8 a piece. I sold a certain number of them. I make
some money. I subtract how much it cost me to get those discs and I
have profit. So there you go. BAM! Last one on this page. You have 400
minutes a month on a cell phone plan. And for some reason you know that on
average you talk two hours a week. Write an expression for this. Well you
start with a big clump of minutes and you burn them off as you talk. So we
start with 400 minutes and as we use them we don't get more minutes
available we get less. Which is a subtraction on the available number
of minutes left. Well I am talking an average of two hours a week, but this is
in hours and the problem starts off in minutes. So I have changed my units of
measure and normally in algebra and mathematics we need all those units to
match. Indeed in this example we will as well. So I am going to take 60
minutes an hour or 2 times 60 for the two hours a week that I talk. I
want this to be in minutes and not in hours. So I have 400 minus 2 times 60 to
convert my hours into minutes times x my number of weeks. Maybe I will just put a w for
week. So my final expression for this one is 400 minus 120 times the number
of weeks that I have on the phone. Now what does this mean? If you are
talking... if you have a 400 minute plan that means it is 400 minutes every single month.
Well if we are talking 2 hours a week, or if we are talking 120 minutes a week,
120, 240, 360, I am adding 120 minutes a week. 120, 240, 360, I think I am going to run out of minutes.
I don't have a big enough plan. So I am Mr. Tarrou. That is a few examples of converting
language into algebraic expressions. Let's look at a few more examples. Let's take a
look at these three examples. The area of a rectangular table that is x inches
wide and 3 times as long as it is wide, make an expression that would describe the
area of that table. Well it is a bit like geometry. It is a rectangle and I always
do pictures. So that means I am going to do a little sketch here. I have a rectangular
table. I don't know how wide it is, so it is x inches wide, but I do know something
about how long it is. However wide it is, its length is going to be 3 times
that. It says write an expression, or I am intending for you to write the expression
of the area of this rectangle. Now I am not going to find the area because I don't
really know how big the table is. But I can write up a little formula or expression
that will give me the area of this table as soon as somebody tells me how wide they
want it. So area of a rectangle is length times width. My length is 3x. My width
is x. Let's not forget, when we multiply like bases we need to add those exponents.
So 3 times x times x is equal to 3x squared and areas in square units, so units squared.
This is just a notation for the unit of measure. It is not a mathematical process.
I am not going to square anything. It is 3x squared and then it is units squared.
So maybe it is like 400 square inches and that would describe its area. The next example,
the measure of the base angles of an isosceles triangle. I am pulling out some examples
here that are going to require you to use some of your background knowledge. An
isosceles triangle is a triangle whose two sides are the same in length. Well if the
base angles opposite those sides must also be equal. Well that is fine and I am not
expecting you. We are not going to be able to actually know what the measurement of
these base angles are, but I can make an expression to describe their measurements.
So we will have this upper angle be some unknown value of x degrees. Well how much
is left for the base angles? Well we also have to know that inside a triangle
the three interior angles have to add up to 180 degrees. So if you have taken up a
certain number of degrees already then what is left is 180 degrees minus x. And these
two base angles are equal so they need to share in the number of degrees that are
left equally. Well if I have $30 and I want to share it equally I do 30 divided
by two which is 15. I probably should have said 30 degrees. I take that and divide
it by two and get 15 so each of the base angles will be 15 degrees. So I am going
to do 180 degrees, the interior sum of a triangle, subtract out this top angle
and divide it by two and that is the number of degrees that is going to equal
or describe each of these base angles. Alright, so that is getting a little bit
more complex. We are returning just a statement in English into an algebraic
expression. I need to know a little bit of properties about triangles though and
isosceles triangles. The sum of two even consecutive integers. Even integers of
course are numbers that can be divided by two. Like two, four, six, eight, ten,
twelve. I keep just adding by two. So let my first integer, my first even
integer, be just x. And my next even integer is going to be whatever this is
plus two. Now if I want to find the sum of these two even consecutive
integers, I am going to put a plus sign here and end up with 2x plus 2. Now you
might be thinking, well how do I know this isn't odd? So I came up with this
example but usually questions about numbers being even or odd will ultimately
say the sum of two even consecutive integers is 28. And it is through the
solution process or the solving process that you end up seeing that the two
answers at the end actually are indeed even or possibly odd. Because the odd
numbers 1, 3, 5, 7, 9 and so on, they are also separated by values of two.
So you might argue that this is a little bit of an incomplete statement or
that this could be even or odd. You know really if you want to verify a
number is even you need to multiply it by two. So maybe you could say that it is
going to be 2x so that I am guaranteed that my unknown value is even and then
add that with the next consecutive even number of 2x plus 2. But I wouldn't
focus on memorizing this too much because usually in an algebraic problem
usually you are doing, when you see this phrase, you are doing an entire equation.
And once you are done solving that equation you will see that your numbers
were either even like they stated or odd like they stated. So most of the
time when you see even consecutive integers it is x and then the next one is
x plus 2. Ok, let's take a look at a few more examples and we will call
this topic done. Now in these last two examples they really sound like a
full question that you should actually come up with a numerical answer for. But
I have given you just enough information so you can set up just an expression
but yet a lot of problem solving skills need to be brought into actually
being able to do these problems. Two cars leave the house going in opposite
directions. Alright, so here is the house. One goes this way, one goes that
way. If one travels twice as fast, hence the arrow that is a little bit
longer. If one travels as fast as the other, how far apart will they be after
four hours? It sounds like you should actually know but it didn't tell you
how fast each car was traveling. So we are just going to get an expression.
Now when you talk about problems where there is stuff in motion, that is
distance equals rate times time. So there is a certain distance that this
car traveled and there is a certain distance that this car traveled. I am
not going to be able to figure out each one, either one, because I don't know how
fast they are going. But, you know, one of these cars I know nothing about.
One car travels as fast as the other one. So the car that traveled the
farthest, I know at least something about their rate. It was twice as fast.
The car that I know nothing about, truck or boat or whatever, I am sure I will not leave
So this distance is the rate times the amount of time that was traveled.
And I know it is four hours. I will fill that back in in a second.
This one though traveled at twice the speed. So even though I have no idea
how fast this car was going, but I do know this car or boat or train or whatever
is twice as fast. So it is 2r instead of 1r. And the amount of time
is going to be the same for that car because it says how far apart are they
after four hours. So both vehicles traveled for four hours or maybe you
walked. I don't know. At any rate it is still motion so it is still distance
equals rate times time. How far apart are they? Well we do know that it was
four hours. So we have 4r for the person going to the left here.
And we have 2 times r times 4 or 8r for the car traveling in the
or the boat, I don't know why I keep saying car, but going in the other direction
twice the speed. How far apart are they? Well if I go two miles this way
and four miles that way, aren't I six miles apart? I want to add those.
So I am going to add these up and my final answer is 12r. They are 12 times
whatever the rate of speed was that maybe ultimately was traveled. You then go
well hey by the way the slow car was going 30 miles an hour or the person
was riding the bike at six miles an hour, the slower one. So with given a
little bit more information we can actually figure out the real distance
but as an expression with an unknown rate of speed they are 12r units apart.
And my last example says that a school sells $3 student tickets and 50 fewer
adult tickets. So in this sentence I actually have not told you how many
student tickets are sold. And then 50 fewer adult tickets are being sold or
some tickets were sold at $3, some tickets were sold at $5. How much money
was collected? Well that sounds like you should have an actual numerical
value but I never told you how many student tickets were sold. But I can
come up with, so I can't actually solve an equation but I can write an
expression to describe what is going on here. You know if I sell five
tickets to students I make $15 right? Three times five. Three, three, three,
three. Every time I sell a ticket I get $3. So it is multiplication for your
sales, how much you brought in. So for student tickets we sold well an
unknown value of x but we made $3 apiece. Now how much money was
collected? Well to that I am going to add how much money I made for my
adult ticket sales. And my adult ticket sales were $5 apiece. And how
many did I sell? I don't know. But I do know that it was 50 fewer than I
sold to the students. So I don't need to come up with a whole new brand
new letter. It is not going to be like 3x plus 5y. You know x for the
student tickets and y for how many adult tickets we sold. Because we know
the relationship. Whenever I sold to the students I sold 50 fewer to the
adults. So here you get to see a little bit of a play of why we had all
those very very simple examples at the beginning of this video. This
simplifies to 3x plus 5x minus 250. Or ultimately these are like terms so
we are going to get 8x minus 250. I am Mr. Tarrou. BAM! Go do your homework.
Write some of those expressions into algebraic expressions.
